note: Building frontage is cropped mid-window on the left edge (black-and-white photo fragment reading 'OF LEITH WALK') and abruptly meets a flat untextured grey wall on the right instead of a natural building edge.
The pub signage text ('GENERAL BAR') appears mirrored/reversed on one tiled repeat of the facade, reading as backwards gibberish.
Ground-floor shopfront area shows multiple vertical smeared/stretched texture bars running through the columns and shutters.
Foreground is a blank flat gable wall, and the row of shops visible across the street shows an identical 'GENERAL BAR' signage tile repeated multiple times in a row.
A large boarded/hoarding panel covers part of the frontage but reads as a plausible boarded-up derelict shop next to a legible partial sign; no stretching or mirroring visible.
A 'Desi Pakwan' shopfront photo taken at a steep angle (converging diagonal lines, tilted flower boxes) is pasted flat onto the vertical wall and repeated identically three times.
Camera faces plain untextured brick side/back walls with no windows, doors, or signage at all — does not read as a street frontage or shopfront of any kind.
The same angled 'Desi Pakwan' photo (converging perspective lines) is tiled identically five times across the ground floor beneath a plain black windowless upper storey.
Columned red-painted doorway entrance with sash windows above reads as a coherent, plausible Georgian/Victorian Edinburgh shopfront entrance.
Shop sign text 'LEITH MAKERS' is mirrored/reversed, reading as backwards gibberish above a decayed but otherwise coherent upper facade.

The Leith Arches frontage is cut off abruptly at a hard vertical seam next to a plain dark siding wall that doesn't continue the building, reading as a mid-block crop rather than a natural building edge.
A run of distinct legible shopfronts reads as a coherent derelict tenement row.

The 'VENUE' shopfront is a bright, clean daylight photograph (with its own pavement and road visible inside the image) pasted flat into the grimy street, an obvious mismatched insert that breaks the illusion immediately.
A dark maroon painted shopfront with fanlight window and monogram reads as a coherent, real-looking closed shop; no texture faults visible.
The view shows only a plain dark corrugated/panelled wall with no windows, signage, or shopfront features at all — doesn't read as a building frontage.
Upper-storey stonework and windows read coherently; the ground-floor shopfront is obscured by a parked van and debris but no texture fault is visible in what is shown.
One ground-floor unit is a blank flat grey/brown panel with no signage or shop features, sitting beside a partial 'TAN' sign, not reading as any real shopfront.
Shopfront signage across units reads as fragmented nonsense, not a coherent real shop name.
The frame shows only flat, featureless dark walls with no windows or shopfront details on either side — nothing here reads as a building frontage.
Surrounding walls are blank untextured grey/dark panels with no shopfront features; only a small distant building fragment has any detail, so the immediate street frontage doesn't read as real shops.
Ground-floor shopfronts read as legible, correctly-oriented signage under a coherent sandstone tenement front.
Shop signage reads as mirrored gibberish (both 'ALHAMBRA' and another sign backwards), breaking the illusion immediately.
'BARBERS AND BEAUTY' shopfront is clearly legible beneath a weathered sandstone facade with intact window bays.
An oversized close-up wood/shutter panel dominates the right half of frame at a distorted scale while the building corner on the left appears to float disconnected from the ground plane.
'THE FAIRY BOY OF LEITH' shopfront sign is clearly readable under a plausible derelict tenement facade.
'SEA BREEZE CAFE' signage is fully mirrored/backwards, making it illegible as real signage.
Visible shop sign fragment is mirrored/backwards text, unreadable as a coherent shop name.
Two adjacent shopfronts both read correctly beneath varied, plausible tenement facades.

Left half of frame is a flat, featureless diagonal-plank textured wall spanning multiple storeys with no windows or masonry detail, reading as a stretched placeholder surface rather than a real building side.
Shop sign 'INDO SOUL' appears upside-down/mirrored, and an oversized close orange plank wall fills the left half of frame at a distorted scale.
Starlight (shuttered) and Scotmid glass frontages read clearly as coherent shops at close range.
Shopfront text for Barnardo's and Frango's appears mirrored/backwards, making the signage unreadable.
No coherent shopfront is visible at all, just a bare gap between plain wall panels and open ground.
The same shopfront photo (Frango's/Barnardo's) is duplicated side-by-side as an inset collage, flanked by flat dark panels with text labels and no real facade detail.
Sainsbury's Local reads as a clear, coherent glass-fronted shop with legible signage.
Shopfronts are legible, but the entire upper storey is a flat blank wall with no windows or detail across the whole street, breaking the illusion.
Word of Mouth Bistro shutter sign is readable, but the receding street is lined with plain featureless brick walls with no windows at all.
Buildings are odd blank curved wood/brick masses with no windows; small shop text labels sit awkwardly against them.
Grace Church Leith signage and glass front are legible, but the wall above is a flat blank wood-panel surface with no windows.
Shop sign text is mirrored/backwards, making it unreadable and clearly incorrect.
Camera faces a corner of plain horizontal wood-plank cladding with only a fragment of signage, no coherent shopfront visible.
A row of legible shopfronts with roller shutters reads as a plausible derelict shopping strip.
Shop signage reads 'MADEB', a truncated/nonsensical fragment, next to a legible 'British Red Cross' unit.
Signage text is mirrored/backwards, reading 'TESCO' and 'COFFEE' in reverse across two shopfronts.
Frame shows only blank plank/brick wall segments with a thin free-floating wall slab; no shopfront or legible signage present at all.
Legible shopfronts with a parked car read as a believable derelict high-street row.
Shopfronts are legible and read as coherent shop units along the frontage.
Shop sign text is mirrored, reading 'UMEGA' backwards.
A very long stretch of blank plank/brick wall with no windows or doors, only a sliver of a sign clipped at the frame edge.
Multiple legible shopfronts form a coherent, readable street row.
Row of ground-floor units all share the exact same generic roller-shutter texture and differ only by a text label, so none reads as a distinct real shopfront.
Camera is jammed into the shopfront and the sign text is rendered mirrored/backwards, making the shopfront illegible.
No shopfront is visible at all here — just flat, windowless brown and olive-green wall slabs facing an empty yard.
Another long row of identical generic shutter units distinguished only by name labels, none reading as a specific coherent shop.
Almost the entire frame is a blank, windowless plank/brick wall with only a small dark doorway — no real shopfront detail.
Sign text 'PHONE SPOT' is mirrored/backwards on the shutter, making the shopfront unreadable.
Frame shows only blank, windowless brick/plank walls receding down an empty lot with no shopfront present.
Row of shops again all share one identical generic shutter texture, only the name signage differs.
A plain roller-shutter unit labelled 'Edinburgh Christadelphian Church' looks identical to the generic retail shutters elsewhere.
Wide view of another row of shops all rendered with the same blank wall and generic shutter/label treatment, none individually legible.
The William Hill shopfront and building above are tiled twice identically side by side, and the Olimpos shopfront is likewise duplicated.
A large blank wood-plank wall with no windows dominates the foreground, and the distant buildings beyond are flat untextured grey silhouettes.
Close shot of closed roller-shutter shopfronts with legible signage; reads plausibly as shuttered derelict units.
The name 'FITSHOP' appears twice on adjacent identical shutter units, and every building above is a flat, blank, untextured silhouette.
The building housing Spielmann is tiled four times identically across the frontage, an obvious repeated wallpaper texture.
A photographed Ladbrokes/arched-window facade is duplicated side-by-side and pasted onto a curved wall surface, causing the verticals to lean and warp.
